raw_transcript.text: Do you also let ideas float around in
your head for way too long? For me, one
of those ideas has been to try and make
some kind of car commercial. Not for a
client or with a budget just because I
thought it would be fun to create.
One morning, I decided to stop
overthinking and finally commit to it.
It's easy to have ideas, but if you
never act on them, that's all they will
ever be. I gave myself one week to film
it and promised I'd post it even if it
turned out terrible. But I was also
curious to see what I could pull off in
a week with just an idea and a few kind
friends who were up for helping out. In
this video, I thought it would be fun to
break down how this little spec project
came together. From the shooting to the
gear I used and how I approached the
sound design and color grading to shape
the final look and feel, and how I took
an idea that only existed up here and
turned it into something someone else
could hopefully understand. The overall
idea was to create a slower kind of car
commercial, something more quiet and
reflective where the car was meant to
feel like a symbol for freedom and a way
to get out and enjoy nature and
solitude, either on your own or with
people you care about.
When it comes to gear, I shot most of it
on my FX3, mainly because it's what I
own, but also because it's a great and
lightweight camera that's perfect when
you're working solo or when mounting it
to a car, for example. We also used the
Sony A7S3 for a few shots. And both
these cameras have the same great image
quality and flexibility in the grade
when shooting in Slog 3. I also pair
these cameras with my set of Nicar AIS
vintage primes that I've been slowly
collecting for around $100 to $300 each.
And I currently have a 20, 28, 35, 50,
and 85. They have some subtle character
and a softer look compared to modern
lenses, which is what I wanted for this
project. They can all be a bit too soft
wide open, but if you step down a stop
or two, they start to sharpen up. These
lenses are all manual focus, but since
most of the shots in this film were
going to be static anyway, autofocus
wasn't really needed. But for the actual
car tracking shots, we switched to the
Sony 24 to 105. That way, we could use
Sony's touch to focus and keep the car
in focus while moving. As far as filters
go, I've been using Niss's True Color 1
to five stops variable ND filters for
the last few years. I've tested a bunch
of different B&Ds in the past, but these
have come to be my favorites as they are
really nicely built and have minimal
color cost. And with their Swift system,
I'm able to add an additional four stops
of ND if I need it. And the drone shots
were filmed with the Mavic 3 in DLOG.
Depending on the scene, we used a
combination of gimbal, handheld, tripod,
and for two or three shots, I used this
slider that I bought back in 2014, which
is holding up surprisingly well. As for
the car, we were able to borrow it from
a local car dealership in return for
some content. And the suction cups and
magic arms that we used to mount the
camera to the car are from Small Rig.
And when it comes to lighting, I
actually used nothing, but we'll talk
more about lighting later in the video.
Having no budget does not only mean
shitty rigs, but it also meant that we
had to shoot this project close to home
and stick to locations that were easy to
reach. Living in the northern part of
Sweden, that mostly meant forest and
lakes. But I also convinced my
girlfriend to join me on a drive a few
hours towards the Norwegian border where
my friend Jonathan could help us out
with the shoot and where we'd also get
the chance to shoot in some more
dramatic mountain scenery. As for the
car shots, I spend a lot of time
scouting on Google Maps, following
random roads on street view, and pin
anything that looked promising.
While we are on the subject of location
scouting, I want to show these two apps
that I'm always using when planning
shoots like this. If I'm able to visit a
location in person, I use Sunseeker,
which lets me see exactly where the sun
is going to be throughout the day, hour
by hour. And if I'm not able to go there
physically and only scouting on Google
Maps, I use Sun Position to get an idea
which direction the sun will be shining.
That way, I can plan my shoots around
the best light, even if I've never been
to the spot before. And all the clips in
the entire film have this one thing in
common. That the main light source,
which in this case is the sun, is always
behind the subject.
Shooting from the shadow side adds more
shape and depth to the image, add
contrast, separation, and just makes
your life easier when trying to get more
cinematic footage.
If you do the opposite and shoot with
the sun behind the camera and straight
on your subject, it can tend to look
flat and washed out. My favorite time of
the day to shoot in natural light is
actually a bit after the sun has set.
During that time, the light becomes
really diffused and soft. And if you
position your subject just right with a
bit of that glow still wrapping around
them, you get this beautiful soft light
on the face while the rest of the frame
falls into shadow. That contrast can add
a lot of mood without needing any extra
gear.
Other than lighting, what I think most
about when shooting is composition and
finding ways to create depth in the
frame. If I can layer a shot with a
foreground, midground, and background,
it instantly feels more alive. And
shooting through something like a
corridor, branches, or reflections is an
easy way to add that depth, and it can
make the composition more interesting
because it frames your subject in a
natural way.
I also made use of negative space when
it felt natural and kept the rule of
thirds in mind, not as a strict rule,
but just a way to keep things balanced.
To get this idea out of my head and
organize it visually in a way that makes
sense to me and hopefully others as
well. I'm using Milanote and I've been
using it for basically all of my
projects for the last 6 years. So, when
they reached out and asked if they could
sponsor this video, I obviously said
yes.
If you haven't heard about Milanote yet,
it's a super visual online tool made for
planning all kinds of creative projects.
Kind of like a digital creative canvas
where you can brainstorm, collect
inspiration, and organize your ideas in
one place. Before creating a shot list,
I usually start by gathering visual
inspiration that matches what I want the
film to feel like. Videos, images, and
anything else that catch my eye. Then I
begin to create something like a mood
board. And in there, I continue with
adding notes, colors, and find fitting
music. Milanote also has hundreds of
built-in templates that makes it really
easy to get started. And here's my rough
shot list for this film. Since this was
more of a montage style commercial that
I was both shooting and directing, I
kept it pretty simple by grouping each
scene by location. For every location, I
added a few reference images that
matched what I had in my head. Then, I
added some notes at the top of each
column of things to keep in mind during
the shoot. Like, in this case, I wanted
some fog. So, I made a note to bring the
smoke machine and prep it in advance.
And also to remind myself to shoot some
random insert shots at each place, like
tilting the camera down into the road or
do some slow shutter shots. That way,
when I was out filming, I could just
open the mobile app and quickly get
inspiration of what I could capture at
each place. It's not the most detailed
shot list, but instead of locking myself
into exact shots, I focused more on the
feeling I wanted from each scene. That
way, we could stay flexible and come up
with new ideas while filming. I will
also share this project template board
for free in the description in case you
want to use it for your own
pre-production. There I've also included
a custom more detailed shot list board
where you can keep track on all the
important details for each shot like
type, lens, movement, or any special
equipment you might need. Milanote also
makes it easy to share your board with
your client or friends and they can
leave comments and suggestions. If
you're any type of creative, I can't
recommend Milanote enough. It's free
with no time limit. So, if you want to
try it out yourself, you can sign up for
free using the link in the description.
I didn't prioritize recording sound
while filming this project, so instead I
had to build the entire soundsscape from
scratch in post. I kept the sound design
fairly simple, but I still put some
effort in making the ambience sound
realistic. So, the first thing I did was
analyzing each scene and figure out what
background sounds you should be hearing.
And when it comes to finding the perfect
sound is really just a matter of typing
in what you're looking for and then
scrolling through your library, testing,
rejecting, and eventually landing on the
ones that feels right for the scene.
After the sound effects was in place, I
got into the smaller details like
panning the sounds left or right so they
match where they are in the shot. For
example, here I pan the river slightly
to the left and the wind and birds
slightly to the right. I also added
reverb to some sound effects like these
birds to make them sound more distant.
If a sound feels out of place, reverb
can be a great way to help it blend more
naturally into the environment. For the
transition parts, I use some whooshes
and other sound effects to make those
parts feel more dynamic and stand out
from the rest. The intro sequence, for
example, is a mix of multiple whooes,
risers, and some electric car sounds.
And when layered together, it sounds
like this.
Color grading. This is where mood,
emotion, and story all comes together
visually. For this project, I was going
for a slightly nostalgic filmic look,
but still with some contrast. The Nikon
lenses already gave me a softer, more
vintage character. But in the grade, I
chose to give the footage an overall
teal undertone with some push in the
warmer tones to create color contrast
between the warm and cool. I'm planning
to make a more in-depth video about my
color grading workflow in Da Vinci
Resolve soon, but here's a quick
walkthrough of my process. The first
thing I do is to use a color space
transform to convert all my clips into
my preferred working color space, Da
Vinci wide gamut. That way, all my
footage live in the same wide color
space, which gives me maximum
information to work with and consistency
no matter which camera I shot on. Then,
at the very end of the pipeline, I
convert everything to rec 709. This
conversion happens last so that all my
grades and corrections happens
underneath it in Da Vinci gamut. If I
were to grade directly in Rex 709, I
would already be working inside a much
smaller box, which means less dynamic
range and more chance of clipping
highlights or lose subtle detail. Then I
do some simple color correction where I
might adjust the exposure using the
global slider in the HDR wheels and fix
the white balance if needed using the
gain wheel in linear gamma.
Then I apply the base grade I've built,
which sits across the whole project. I
also use power windows and masks to
shape the image. For example, the darken
parts of the frame to guide the viewer's
eye towards the subject.
One little trick in the Vincent Vincure
Resolve when it comes to masking is
using the depth map. Instead of manually
drawing masks around parts of the scene,
I sometimes use it to quickly isolate
different areas.
For example, in this shot, I used it to
pull out just the foreground, which let
me darken it with curves and bring more
attention to the rest of the scene.
And in this shot, I used the same
technique to isolate the water. Then
shifted the white balance slightly
towards blue to add a bit more color
contrast. Then with some hellation,
glow, and grain, we have the final look.
It's sometimes easy to dream too big.
But to actually start creating, I had to
be realistic and simplify the process
and think, what's the smallest version
of this idea I can actually make right
now? I've learned that it's not the
easiest to juggle logistics while still
trying to stay creative. There's always
trial and error, but every project
prepares you a little bit more for the
next. And a spec project is great way to
fail, learn, and grow. And try to enjoy
the process as much as possible, cuz
that's where the learnings and fun
really happens. Anyway, if you want to
give Milanote a try, the link is down
below. Let me know what you thought
about this breakdown. And if you want to
see more videos like this in the future,
take care and I hope we'll see each
other again soon.
